definition: Up to date with the latest information or developments on a subject.
example: The manager held weekly briefings to keep the team abreast of regulatory changes.
mnemonic: Stay ABREAST = keep your chest forward, facing the latest news.

definition: To flee secretly, especially to escape legal consequences or detection.
example: The embezzler absconded with the funds before auditors could freeze the accounts.
mnemonic: AB (away) + SCOND (hide) — disappear into hiding, away from the scene.

definition: To make a feature or quality more pronounced or noticeable; to emphasize.
example: The lighting in the gallery was designed to accentuate the texture of each sculpture.
mnemonic: ACCENT + uate — put the accent mark on what matters most.

definition: Formed or devised for a particular purpose or situation, without prior planning.
example: The committee formed an ad hoc subgroup to address the unexpected budget shortfall.
mnemonic: Latin 'for this' — invented ON THE SPOT for THIS one situation.

definition: To follow or observe a rule, agreement, or obligation consistently and loyally.
example: All participants must adhere to the terms of the non-disclosure agreement.
mnemonic: ADHERE = AD (to) + HERE — stick to it, stay right here with the rule.
confusableNote: Do not confuse with 'cohere' (to form a logical whole); adhere implies commitment to an external standard.

definition: Willing to cooperate or comply; open to suggestion or persuasion.
example: The director was amenable to revising the timeline after hearing the team's concerns.
mnemonic: AMENABLE sounds like 'able to say Amen' — ready to agree and go along.
confusableNote: Do not confuse with 'amiable' (friendly in manner); amenable stresses willingness to comply, not general warmth.

definition: Strong hostility or active ill will directed toward another person or group.
example: Years of border disputes had bred deep animosity between the two neighboring nations.
mnemonic: ANIMAL + osity — raw, animal-like hatred boiling inside.

definition: Hostile intent or deep-seated ill will; an antagonistic attitude toward someone.
example: The judge noted that the plaintiff's lawsuit was motivated by personal animus rather than genuine legal grievance.
mnemonic: ANIMUS — the Latin root for 'spirit' gone dark: a spirit of hatred.
confusableNote: Do not confuse with 'anima' (soul/inner self in Jungian psychology); animus specifically denotes hostile intent.

definition: One who argues in defense or justification of a doctrine, policy, or person.
example: The historian was branded an apologist for the regime because he downplayed its atrocities.
mnemonic: APOLOGIST ≠ apologizer — this person DEFENDS, not regrets.
confusableNote: Do not confuse with 'apology' (expression of regret); an apologist offers intellectual defense, not personal remorse.

definition: To formally notify or inform someone of relevant facts or developments.
example: The embassy apprised its citizens of the travel restrictions before the summit began.
mnemonic: APPRISE = bring a PRIZE of information to someone who needs it.
confusableNote: Do not confuse with 'appraise' (to assess value); apprise means to inform, not evaluate.

definition: Suitable or fitting for a particular purpose, context, or occasion.
example: Formal attire is appropriate for the board's annual dinner.
mnemonic: APPRO- (approved) + PRIATE — what has been approved as right for the moment.
confusableNote: Do not confuse with the verb 'appropriate' (to take for one's own use), which carries a different and sometimes negative meaning.

definition: Exactly right or suitable for a given situation; well-chosen and fitting.
example: Her apt analogy made the complex economic theory immediately understandable.
mnemonic: APT is short and sharp — a short, sharp word for the perfectly short and sharp choice.

definition: Requiring great effort and endurance; extremely demanding and difficult.
example: The researchers completed an arduous two-year fieldwork campaign in the polar region.
mnemonic: ARDUOUS sounds like 'hard you us' — hard for all of us to get through.

definition: Without guile or artifice; naturally simple, sincere, and free from pretense.
example: Her artless confession disarmed the cynical negotiators who had expected clever maneuvering.
mnemonic: ART-LESS — lacking the art of deception; what you see is genuinely what you get.
confusableNote: Do not confuse with 'artless' meaning 'lacking artistic skill'; the GRE sense is 'free from cunning or affectation.'

definition: Harshly severe or sharply critical in effect; causing a tightening or constricting reaction.
example: The critic's astringent review stripped the novel of every unearned claim to greatness.
mnemonic: ASTRINGENT tightens and stings — its words contract and bite like a harsh tonic.

definition: To weaken or reduce in force, density, or severity; to make thin or slender.
example: The medication attenuated the severity of the patient's symptoms without eliminating them entirely.
mnemonic: ATTENUATE — think 'thin it out': at-THIN-uate the signal until it fades.
confusableNote: Do not confuse with 'extenuate' (to lessen the seriousness of an offense); attenuate focuses on physically or quantitatively weakening something.

definition: Providing supplementary or additional support to a primary effort or organization.
example: The hospital's auxiliary staff volunteers helped maintain operations during the nursing shortage.
mnemonic: AUX (extra) + ILIARY — the auxiliary cable is the EXTRA plug when the main one fails.

definition: To assert or affirm something confidently and with conviction as true.
example: The witness averred that she had seen the defendant at the scene at midnight.
mnemonic: AVER = A + VER (truth) — to speak truth with certainty.
confusableNote: Do not confuse with 'avert' (to turn away or prevent); aver is purely a speech act of affirmation.

definition: To prevent an undesirable event from occurring; to turn away or ward off.
example: Swift diplomatic negotiations averted what might have become a full-scale military conflict.
mnemonic: AVERT = A + VERT (turn) — turn danger away before it arrives.
confusableNote: Do not confuse with 'averse' (having a strong dislike); avert is an action of prevention, not a feeling.

definition: Owing a debt of gratitude or obligation to someone for a favor or benefit received.
example: Having accepted the grant, the researcher felt beholden to publish results favorable to the sponsor.
mnemonic: BEHOLDEN — be HELD by obligation, as if someone's hand holds you in place.

definition: To give a false impression of something; to contradict or misrepresent.
example: Her calm expression belied the intense anxiety she felt before the performance.
mnemonic: BE + LIE — to belie is to make something BE a LIE by contradicting the truth.

definition: To dismiss or disparage someone or something as unimportant or inferior.
example: The senior engineer belittled the intern's proposal without even reading it thoroughly.
mnemonic: BE + LITTLE — to make someone feel small and insignificant.

definition: Lacking something needed or valued; desolately deprived of or grieving a loss.
example: Bereft of funding, the research program was quietly discontinued after a decade of progress.
mnemonic: BEREFT sounds like 'bereaved left' — left behind, robbed of what you had.

definition: Showing a casual, unconcerned indifference to the consequences of one's actions.
example: His blithe disregard for deadlines exasperated colleagues who had to cover for him.
mnemonic: BLITHE — sounds like 'blythe' — breezy and light, blissfully untroubled by reality.
confusableNote: Note that 'blithe' in the GRE context often carries a negative connotation of careless indifference, not innocent happiness.

definition: Noisily energetic and rowdy; unruly and turbulent in a way that provokes or disrupts.
example: The boisterous crowd outside the stadium made it impossible to hear the opening announcement.
mnemonic: BOISTEROUS — BOILing + STIR = the pot is boiling and stirring up chaos.

definition: To persuade someone by flattery, coaxing, or gentle persistent urging.
example: She cajoled her reluctant colleague into presenting at the conference with lavish compliments.
mnemonic: CA-JOLE — jolly someone along with sweet words until they give in.

definition: To cease resistance and surrender, especially under agreed conditions.
example: After months of negotiations, the company capitulated to the union's demands for higher wages.
mnemonic: CAPITULATE — think 'capital falls': when the capital surrenders, the war is over.
confusableNote: Do not confuse with 'recapitulate' (to summarize); capitulate means to give up resistance entirely.

definition: Excessively critical of trivial faults; inclined to find fault unreasonably.
example: The captious editor rejected the manuscript over minor punctuation inconsistencies.
mnemonic: CAPTIOUS — trying to CAPTURE you in a fault; always hunting for something to criticize.
confusableNote: Do not confuse with 'capricious' (impulsively unpredictable); captious specifically means fault-finding and critical.

definition: To officially examine and suppress or remove material deemed objectionable or inappropriate.
example: The government censored news reports that contradicted the official account of the incident.
mnemonic: CENSOR — a sensor that detects and cuts out what authority doesn't want you to see.
confusableNote: Do not confuse with 'censure' (to formally criticize or condemn); censor involves removal or suppression of content.

definition: Excessively sweet, sentimental, or flattering to the point of causing disgust or revulsion.
example: The film's cloying sentimentality made it difficult to watch past the first act.
mnemonic: CLOYING — too much CLOY (sweetness) CLOGS your senses until you feel sick.

definition: To treat with excessive care and indulgence; to pamper or overprotect.
example: Critics argued that the policy coddled underperforming divisions instead of holding them accountable.
mnemonic: CODDLE — like coddled eggs, kept in warm gentle water, never exposed to the full heat.

definition: To express or feel sympathy and sorrow for another's misfortune; to sympathize.
example: After the team's loss, the coach commiserated with the players rather than criticizing their effort.
mnemonic: COM (together) + MISERATE (misery) — share in misery together.

definition: Powerfully persuasive; evoking strong interest or conviction that demands attention.
example: The prosecutor presented compelling evidence that left the jury little room for reasonable doubt.
mnemonic: COMPELLING — COMPELS you forward; you can't look away or disagree.

definition: Smugly self-satisfied and uncritically content, often unaware of potential risks.
example: The market leader grew complacent and failed to anticipate the disruptive startup's rise.
mnemonic: COM + PLACENT (pleased) — pleased with the status quo and blind to its dangers.
confusableNote: Do not confuse with 'complaisant' (eager to please others); complacent means pleased with oneself.

definition: To accept or allow behavior considered wrong; to overlook or implicitly approve an offense.
example: The school board was accused of condoning bullying by failing to enforce its own policies.
mnemonic: CONDONE — CONDOnE sounds like 'condone done' — let the wrong deed pass as done and forgiven.

definition: General agreement reached among all members of a group after deliberation.
example: After lengthy debate, the panel reached a consensus on the proposed safety standards.
mnemonic: CON (together) + SENSUS (feeling) — everyone feeling the same way together.

definition: Standing out strikingly so as to attract notice; clearly visible or prominent.
example: The artist's conspicuous talent was evident from her very first public exhibition.
mnemonic: CON-SPIC-UOUS — your eyes CONSPIRE to SPEC (spy) something so obvious it can't be missed.

definition: To assert or maintain a position in argument; to compete or struggle against opposition.
example: The defense attorney contended that the evidence had been mishandled and should be excluded.
mnemonic: CONTEND — CONTest + TEND to your argument; keep tending the debate.

definition: To act in violation of a law, rule, or prohibition; to conflict with or infringe.
example: The new executive order appeared to contravene several provisions of the existing statute.
mnemonic: CONTRA (against) + VENE (come) — come against the rule and break it.
confusableNote: Do not confuse with 'contradict' (to assert the opposite verbally); contravene implies active violation of a law or rule.

definition: Feeling deep remorse or regret for one's wrongdoing; sincerely penitent.
example: The contrite executive publicly apologized and donated his bonus to the affected employees.
mnemonic: CONTRITE — CONT + RITE — going through the RITE of penitence to atone for wrong.

definition: A confusing or difficult problem or question that is hard to solve or understand.
example: The paradox of economic growth causing environmental harm presented a conundrum for policymakers.
mnemonic: CONUNDRUM — sounds like CONUNDR-UM… the 'um' at the end is you stumped, uncertain what to say.

definition: Based on or conforming to accepted standards or common practice; typical and expected.
example: The startup rejected the conventional business model in favor of a subscription-based approach.
mnemonic: CONVENTION + AL — following what is done at conventions, the established norm.

definition: A firmly held belief or opinion argued with great certainty and persuasive force.
example: She spoke with such conviction that even her skeptical audience reconsidered their position.
mnemonic: CONVICT + ION — like a court verdict, a conviction makes the belief legally settled in your mind.
confusableNote: Note the dual meaning: in the GRE persuasion sense, conviction is the force of belief; the legal sense (being found guilty) is distinct.

definition: Large, heavy, or unwieldy; so complicated or burdensome as to be difficult to use.
example: The cumbersome approval process delayed the project by nearly three months.
mnemonic: CUMBER + SOME — a CUCUMBER that's somehow SOME-thing you have to lug around: heavy and awkward.
